content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 2 Corinthians Chapter 5 where the three translators produced meaningfully different renderings.

---

### v.1 — "earthly house of the tent" vs. "tent that is our earthly home" vs. "earthly tent we live in"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if the earthly house of the tent of us is destroyed" | Literal double metaphor; preserves genitive chain |
| LOGOS | "if the tent that is our earthly home is destroyed" | Simplifies genitive chain; "home" for "house" |
| PNEUMA | "when this earthly tent we live in is taken down (that is, when we die and leave this earthly body)" | Adds parenthetical explanation; "when" replaces "if" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EMET's genitive chain is opaque. PNEUMA changes the conditional ("if") to temporal ("when") and adds an interpretive gloss not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA converts conditional to temporal and adds explanation.

---

### v.3 — "if indeed...not be found naked" — textual issue

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if indeed, having put [it] on, we will not be found naked" | Follows Sinaiticus ἐνδυσάμενοι ("having put on") |
| LOGOS | "so that by putting it on we may not be found naked" | Same reading; purpose clause |
| PNEUMA | "For we will put on heavenly bodies; we will not be spirits without bodies" | Interprets "naked" as "disembodied"; two declarative statements |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's interpretation ("spirits without bodies") is exegetically plausible but goes beyond the text. "Naked" (γυμνοί) in this context likely means disembodied, but Paul leaves the metaphor intact.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA resolves ambiguity Paul leaves open.

---

### v.4 — "unclothed...clothed upon" vs. "die and get rid of...put on"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we do not wish to be unclothed but to be clothed upon, that the mortal may be swallowed up by the life" | Preserves clothing metaphor |
| LOGOS | "not that we would be unclothed, but that we would be further clothed, so that what is mortal may be swallowed up by life" | "Further clothed" — adds "further" |
| PNEUMA | "it's not that we want to die and get rid of these bodies that clothe us. Rather, we want to put on our new bodies so that these dying bodies will be swallowed up by life" | Full paraphrase; "die" interprets "unclothed" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Further clothed" (ἐπενδύσασθαι) is actually correct — the compound verb means "to put on over" (clothing over clothing). PNEUMA's expansion makes the theology explicit but replaces the metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved. LOGOS captures the compound verb's meaning well.

---

### v.10 — "worthless" vs. "evil" vs. "evil we have done"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whether good or worthless" | φαῦλον as "worthless/bad" |
| LOGOS | "whether good or evil" | φαῦλον as "evil" |
| PNEUMA | "whatever we deserve for the good or evil we have done in this earthly body" | Adds "deserve" and "earthly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. φαῦλος can mean "worthless," "bad," or "evil." "Evil" is stronger than the Greek warrants (φαῦλος is milder than κακός or πονηρός), but "worthless" (EMET) may be too mild. LOGOS's "evil" is the conventional rendering.

**Status:** Resolved. Degree of moral severity differs.

---

### v.11 — "fear of the Lord" — "persuade people" vs. "persuade others" vs. "work hard to persuade others"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "knowing the fear of the Lord, we persuade people" | φόβον as "fear"; πείθομεν as "persuade" |
| LOGOS | "knowing the fear of the Lord, we persuade others" | Same; "others" for "people" |
| PNEUMA | "Because we understand our fearful responsibility to the Lord, we work hard to persuade others" | "Fearful responsibility" for φόβος; adds "work hard" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "fearful responsibility" interprets φόβος — which means "fear/reverence" — as "responsibility," shifting from awe to duty.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA softens "fear" into "responsibility."

---

### v.12 — "boasting in face and not in heart" vs. "outward appearance...what is in the heart"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [ones] boasting in face and not in heart" | Literal ἐν προσώπῳ / ἐν καρδίᾳ |
| LOGOS | "those who boast about outward appearance and not about what is in the heart" | Interprets πρόσωπον as "outward appearance" |
| PNEUMA | "those who brag about having a spectacular ministry rather than having a sincere heart" | Full interpretive expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Outward appearance" captures πρόσωπον ("face/surface") well. PNEUMA's "spectacular ministry" and "sincere heart" are interpretations that go well beyond the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds specificity not in the text.

---

### v.14 — "constrains" vs. "controls" vs. "controls"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the love of the Christos constrains us" | συνέχει as "constrains/compresses" |
| LOGOS | "the love of Christ controls us" | "Controls" — active direction |
| PNEUMA | "Christ's love controls us" | Same as LOGOS; possessive form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS ("controls"). συνέχω means "to hold together, press in upon, constrain." "Controls" captures the directing force. EMET's "constrains" preserves the idea of compression/confinement but sounds restrictive in English.

**Status:** Resolved. Nuance of συνέχω — compressive force vs. directing control.

---

### v.16 — "according to flesh" vs. "according to the flesh" vs. "from a human point of view"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "know no one according to flesh...known Christos according to flesh" | Literal κατὰ σάρκα without article |
| LOGOS | "regard no one according to the flesh...regarded Christ according to the flesh" | Adds article; "regard" for "know" |
| PNEUMA | "stopped evaluating others from a human point of view...thought of Christ merely from a human point of view" | "Human point of view" for κατὰ σάρκα; adds "merely" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. κατὰ σάρκα is a technical Pauline phrase meaning "according to the flesh/by human standards." PNEUMA's "human point of view" captures the sense but loses the characteristically Pauline vocabulary.

**Status:** Resolved. κατὰ σάρκα is a key Pauline phrase worth preserving.

---

### v.17 — "new creation" vs. "new person" / "new life"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if anyone [is] in Christos, [there is] a new creation; the old [things] have passed away; behold, new [things] have come" | καινὴ κτίσις as "new creation"; cosmic scope |
| LOGOS | "if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come" | Same term; "he is" |
| PNEUMA | "anyone who belongs to Christ has become a new person. The old life is gone; a new life has begun!" | "New person" for κτίσις; "old life" / "new life" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "new person" reduces κτίσις ("creation") to individual transformation. Paul's phrase is cosmic — a "new creation" has begun, not just a "new person." Similarly, "old life / new life" narrows "the old / the new" to individual biography. Paul means the old order of existence.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "New creation" (cosmic, eschatological) vs. "new person" (individual, biographical) — fundamental to Pauline theology.

---

### v.19 — "in Christ God was reconciling" vs. "God was in Christ, reconciling"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "God was in Christos reconciling [the] world to himself" | θεὸς ἦν ἐν Χριστῷ as a unit |
| LOGOS | "in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself" | "In Christ" as instrumental/spherical |
| PNEUMA | "God was in Christ, reconciling the world to himself" | "God was in Christ" as incarnational statement |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The syntax is genuinely ambiguous. LOGOS reads ἐν Χριστῷ as the sphere of God's reconciling action ("in Christ, God was reconciling"). EMET/PNEUMA read θεὸς ἦν ἐν Χριστῷ as "God was in Christ" — a statement about divine presence. Both are grammatically possible; LOGOS is more consistent with Paul's typical use of ἐν Χριστῷ as a prepositional phrase modifying the verb.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. The syntactic ambiguity affects whether this is a statement about incarnation ("God was in Christ") or about the sphere of reconciliation ("in Christ, God was reconciling").

---

### v.20 — "be reconciled to God" vs. "Come back to God!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "be reconciled to God" | Aorist passive imperative; formal |
| LOGOS | "be reconciled to God" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Come back to God!" | Dynamic paraphrase; adds exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS. "Be reconciled" preserves the passive voice — the emphasis is on receiving God's action, not on human initiative. PNEUMA's "come back" shifts to active human agency and sounds more like an altar call than an apostolic proclamation.

**Status:** Resolved. Active vs. passive voice changes the theology of reconciliation.

---

### v.21 — "he made sin" vs. "he made him to be sin" vs. "the offering for our sin"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] not knowing sin, on behalf of us he made sin" | Direct; ἁμαρτίαν ἐποίησεν as "made sin" |
| LOGOS | "he made him to be sin who knew no sin" | "To be sin" — identity statement |
| PNEUMA | "God made Christ, who never sinned, to be the offering for our sin" | "Offering for our sin" — interprets ἁμαρτία as sin offering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says ἁμαρτίαν ἐποίησεν — "he made [him] sin." Not "sin offering," though that meaning of ἁμαρτία exists in the LXX (Lev 4-5). PNEUMA's "offering for our sin" resolves an ambiguity Paul may have intended to preserve. The starkness of "made him to be sin" should stand.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "Made him sin" vs. "made him a sin offering" — fundamental atonement theology. PNEUMA's reading is exegetically possible (ἁμαρτία = sin offering in sacrificial contexts) but narrows Paul's deliberately shocking statement.

---

*Summary: 13 disagreements logged. Three flagged ⚠️: v.17 ("new creation" vs. "new person" — cosmic vs. individual), v.19 (syntactic ambiguity of "in Christ God was reconciling"), v.21 ("made sin" vs. "sin offering" — atonement theology). Other significant: v.14 (συνέχω — "constrains" vs. "controls"), v.16 (κατὰ σάρκα), v.20 (passive vs. active voice in reconciliation).*

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
